quotes.verbatim_quote:
The basic application of a
tritone chord substitution is to take any 7th chord and play another 7th chord that has its root a tritone away
from the original.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the foundational definition of the technique the entire chapter is built around — without it, none of the subsequent applications make sense.

quotes.topic: Tritone sub over II V I
quotes.verbatim_quote:
This guitar lesson demonstrates how you can play scales and arpeggios starting from the b5
(a tritone away) of the V7 chord in a II V I chord progression. This way you will highlight altered tones as the
b9 and the #11.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage establishes the core method — using the substitution to target specific altered tones — which governs every lick and scale choice that follows.

quotes.topic: II V I tritone sub example
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Let’s take a C major II VI chord progression Dm7 | G7 | CM7 | % | After applying a tritone substitution to
the V7 chord of a II V I chord progression, we get the following sequence Dm7 | Db7 | CM7 | % | The idea
behind is to play Db over G7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the canonical worked example that concretely applies the abstract definition to a specific progression, giving the reader a template they can transpose to any key.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The root of Db7 highlights the #11/b5 of G7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage maps each tone of the Db7 arpeggio to its function within G7, teaching the student exactly which altered colors are produced by option one.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The second option is to play a dominant 9 . .
arpeggio (1 - 3-5 -b7-9) which means that Dominant 9 Arpeggio Shapes
you have to add the ninth to the previous 3 0 a ® ® 9
dom7 arpeggio.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the extension from dom7 to dom9 as the incremental step for option two, adding the b13 color to the palette available over G7.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The third option consists in playing a major
pentatonic scale (1 - 2 - 3-5-6).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the major pentatonic formula as option three, anchoring subsequent tone analysis to a specific scale formula.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The sixth (Bb) of Db7 should be used as an
approach note in order to target a chord tone
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a concrete avoidance rule — Bb must not rest on a chord tone but rather approach one — a critical guideline for idiomatic phrasing over the substitution.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The fourth option to solo with a tritone

sub is to use the Mixolydian mode (1 - 2 . .
-3-4-5-6-b7). Ithas the same note as Mixolydian Mode Shapes

the major pentatonic scale with a fourth  ~—-@ @ rp 5) ©
(4/11) and a seventh (7).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Completes the four-option framework by defining Mixolydian and relating it to the already-known pentatonic — a pedagogical bridge that reduces the cognitive load of learning the new option.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The seventh of Db7 (B) is the third of G7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives two specific rules about which scale degrees to avoid as resting tones and which to target, shaping how Mixolydian is applied in practice.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Bar 1: Dm7 arpeggio (D - F - A - C)
Bar 2 : Descending Db7 arpeggio (Db - F - A - Cb)
Bar 3 : CM7 arpeggio (C - E- G- B)
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the first fully notated example applying option one across all three bars of the II V I, showing exactly how arpeggio choices are assigned measure-by-measure.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Playing Db pentatonic over G7 chord provides some interesting colors:
Db is the #11 of the G7 chord.
Eb is the b13 of G7.
F is the minor 7th of G7.
Ab is the b9 of G7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides a complete tone-by-tone analysis of Db major pentatonic against G7, giving the student a reusable reference for why this scale sounds idiomatic over an altered dominant.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Playing Db9 arpeggio on G7 highlights the following tones :

Db is the #11 of the G7 chord.

F is the minor 7th of G7.
Ab is the b9 of G7.
Cb is the major third of G7.
Eb is the b13 of G7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the tone-mapping method to the dom9 arpeggio, reinforcing the analytical framework across a second option so students can generalize the approach.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Measure 2 : Db9 arpeggio (Eb - Cb - Ab - F - Db) and chromaticism to approach the root of CM7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explicitly names chromaticism as a device used to connect the tritone-sub bar to the tonic resolution, teaching a specific voice-leading technique.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Measure 1 : D Dorian bebop (D - E - F - F# - G- A- B- C). The Dorian bebop scale is the Dorian mode with

a passing tone.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the Dorian bebop scale by specifying its formula and explaining that F# is a chromatic passing tone added to Dorian — a distinct scale concept introduced only in the final lick.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Measure 2 : Db7 mixolydian mode ( Db - Eb - F - Gb - Ab - Bb - Cb).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Demonstrates option four (Mixolydian) in a concrete lick context, completing the cycle so every one of the four options introduced earlier has been illustrated in a musical example.
